audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Exodus / Shemot text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Moses' birth/rescue, women's agency, Egyptian violence, Midian, covenant remembrance, Divine hearing.

verses.restored: A man from house of Levi went and took a daughter of Levi.
The woman conceived and bore a son; she saw that he was good, and she hid him three months.
When she could no longer hide him, she took for him an ark/basket of papyrus, smeared it with bitumen and pitch, placed the child in it, and set it among reeds by bank of Nile.
His sister stood at a distance to know what would be done to him.
The daughter of Pharaoh came down to bathe at the Nile, and her young women walked beside the Nile. She saw the basket among the reeds and sent her female servant, and she took it.
She opened and saw him, the child, and look, a boy weeping; she had compassion on him and said, This is one of the Hebrews' children.
His sister said to Pharaoh's daughter, Shall I go and call for you a nursing woman from the Hebrew women, so she may nurse the child for you?
Pharaoh's daughter said to her, Go. The young woman went and called the child's mother.
Pharaoh's daughter said to her, Take this child and nurse him for me, and I myself will give your wages. The woman took the child and nursed him.
The child grew, and she brought him to Pharaoh's daughter, and he became her son. She called his name Moses, saying, Because from the waters I drew him out.
In those days, when Moses had grown, he went out to his brothers and saw their burdens; he saw an Egyptian man striking a Hebrew man, one of his brothers.
He turned here and there, and saw no man, and struck the Egyptian and hid him in the sand.
He went out the second day, and look, two Hebrew men were fighting. He said to the guilty one, Why do you strike your companion?
He said, Who set you as man, prince, and judge over us? Are you saying to kill me as you killed the Egyptian? Moses feared and said, Surely the matter is known.
Pharaoh heard this matter and sought to kill Moses. Moses fled from face of Pharaoh and dwelt in land of Midian; he sat down by a well.
Now the priest of Midian had seven daughters. They came and drew water and filled troughs to water their father's flock.
The shepherds came and drove them away, but Moses rose and saved/helped them and watered their flock.
They came to Reuel their father, and he said, Why have you hurried to come today?
They said, An Egyptian man rescued us from hand of shepherds, and also drew water for us and watered the flock.
He said to his daughters, Where is he? Why did you leave the man? Call him, that he may eat bread.
Moses was willing to dwell with the man, and he gave Zipporah his daughter to Moses.
She bore a son, and he called his name Gershom, for he said, I have been a sojourner in a foreign land.
In those many days, king of Egypt died. The children of Israel groaned from the service, and they cried out; their cry for help went up to the Divine from the service.
The Divine heard their groaning, and the Divine remembered his covenant with Abraham, with Isaac, and with Jacob.
The Divine saw the children of Israel, and the Divine knew.

translation_notes: Moses' basket uses tevah/ark language, echoing Noah: life preserved through waters.
Women's agency drives the rescue: mother, sister, Pharaoh's daughter, female servant, and later Midianite daughters.
Moses' first act of resistance is morally complicated and should not be romanticized as clean liberation violence.
Divine hearing/remembering/seeing/knowing forms the turning point.
safety_note: The chapter shows both courageous rescue and dangerous impulsive violence. It should not be used to sanctify vigilantism. The deeper liberation movement begins with Divine hearing and covenant remembrance.
